chunk_summary: Counseling adolescents on protein powders, creatine, and caffeine products focuses on harm reduction, food-first approaches, and managing risks like contamination, adverse effects, and anti-doping concerns.
learning_objectives: Understand the risks and benefits of protein powders, creatine, and caffeine products in adolescent nutrition.
Learn practical counseling strategies to promote food-first approaches and harm reduction.
Identify safety guidelines for supplement use, including contamination risks and adverse effects.
key_facts: Protein powders should only supplement meals when dietary protein targets cannot be met; avoid multi-ingredient blends.
Routine creatine use is not recommended for minors due to limited long-term safety data; stop use if muscle cramps or renal concerns arise.
Adolescents should limit caffeine intake to <100 mg/day and avoid energy drinks due to risks like palpitations and sleep disruption.
Third-party testing reduces but does not eliminate contamination and anti-doping risks in competitive athletes.
Energy drinks marketed in India often contain high sugar and caffeine, increasing health risks.
reference_quote: Most performance gains in adolescents come from adequate sleep, regular meals, hydration, and periodized training. (Source: IOC consensus statement)
document_key_topics: Creatine use and safety in adolescents
Caffeine and energy drink risks in adolescent health
